one_line_takeaway: AJailBench is the first open benchmark showing that signal-level, semantically-preserved audio perturbations (optimized via Bayesian search) reliably and significantly worsen jailbreak resistance across seven leading audio-language models, including GPT-4o and Gemini, exposing an acoustic attack surface beyond text prompts.
key_finding: No evaluated LAM is robust across all safety dimensions, and small, semantically-preserved audio perturbations further and significantly degrade safety. On AJailBench-Base (Table 2 averages), safety ranges widely: SpeechGPT is least aligned (ASR 0.945), GPT-4o is strongest (ASR 0.190), and SALMONN is extremely strict (ASR 0.137) but at the cost of usability (frequently unhelpful, Relevance only 1.531). On the base-vs-APT+ comparison (Table 3), APT+ perturbations raise ASR for every model with statistically significant degradation (paired t-test p<0.01): GPT-4o 0.235->0.314, Gemini-flash 0.611->0.737, DiVA 0.580->0.674, Qwen2-Audio 0.491->0.526, SALMONN 0.356->0.433. Time-stretch and fade perturbations were the most frequently selected by Bayesian optimization and had the strongest robustness-degrading effect. The authors also note LAMs largely mirror the safety behavior of their underlying text LLMs, and that GPT-4o often deflects with neutral non-refusal language rather than issuing hard refusals.
core_contribution: AJailBench, presented as the first open-source benchmark for systematically evaluating jailbreak vulnerabilities in Large Audio-Language Models (LAMs). The paper's own contribution has two parts: (1) AJailBench-Base, a static dataset of 1,495 adversarial audio prompts spanning 10 policy-violating categories, produced by converting text jailbreak prompts to speech via realistic TTS; and (2) the Audio Perturbation Toolkit (APT), a set of 7 signal-level perturbations across time-, frequency-, and mixing-domains, combined with a Semantic Consistency Constraint (GPTScore + human threshold) and Bayesian optimization to produce AJailBench-APT+, a dynamically optimized adversarial dataset. The authors position their work against prior audio-jailbreak studies (Yang et al. 2024, Hughes et al. 2024/BoN, Xiao et al. 2025) in Table 1, claiming they are unique in offering a signal-level perturbation benchmark WITH semantic-consistency constraints, combinable attacks (via Bayesian optimization), and full open-source release of both benchmark and tool. Note: SpeechGPT/SALMONN/Qwen2-Audio/GPT-4o etc. are evaluated targets and cited prior systems, not contributions of this paper.
threat_model: Black-box, query-based adversarial jailbreak of deployed LAMs. The adversary starts from textual jailbreak prompts (collected from papers/Reddit and auto-generation tools, filtered to already bypass ChatGPT-3.5/4), synthesizes them to speech via TTS, and then applies subtle signal-level audio perturbations (amplitude/energy scaling, trimming, fade, pitch shift, temporal scaling, ultra/infrasound priming, natural-noise injection) that a human still perceives as the same intelligible request. The APT+ optimization loop queries the target model M for its response and minimizes a SentenceBERT refusal-similarity score, so it assumes query access to model outputs (public APIs for GPT-4o/Gemini; local inference for open-source models). The goal is to steer the model away from refusal toward harmful completions while preserving the original harmful intent, exploiting the hypothesis that LAM safety alignment over-relies on clean transcribed-speech representations and misses non-canonical acoustic patterns.
limitations: By the authors' own Appendix C: (1) they do NOT investigate or evaluate any defenses against audio adversarial attacks (the 'Defense' section only proposes future directions such as adversarial fine-tuning, consistency regularization, and front-end filtering, none implemented or tested); (2) the study is English-only, and cross-lingual robustness is unexplored despite accent diversity. Reviewer-observed limits: ASR is computed by refusal-keyword matching, a coarse proxy that can misclassify neutral-deflection responses (which the paper itself observes GPT-4o produces); the semantic threshold rests on a small human study (3 raters, 150 samples); Table 2 (per-category averages) and Table 3 (base vs APT+ subset) report noticeably different base ASR values for the same models (e.g., SALMONN 0.137 vs 0.356), suggesting the APT+ comparison uses a different/sampled subset, which the paper does not fully reconcile; and the strongest closed models still show only modest absolute ASR increases under APT+.

datasets_used: AJailBench-Base: 1,495 adversarial audio prompts across 10 policy-violating categories (authors' own; TTS-synthesized from filtered text jailbreaks)
AJailBench-APT+: extended dataset of Bayesian-optimized, signal-level perturbed adversarial audio (authors' own)
Source text jailbreaks collected from published papers, Reddit-shared prompts, and JailbreakBench-style auto-generation tools (Chao et al. 2024; Shen et al. 2023), filtered to bypass ChatGPT-3.5/4
AdvBench referenced as the outdated basis of prior audio-jailbreak work (not the basis of this benchmark)
Human intelligibility study corpus: 150 audio samples rated by 3 undergraduate evaluators
quantitative_results: 1,495 adversarial audio prompts; 10 policy-violating categories; 118 timbres; 4 English accents
Base ASR averages (Table 2, lower=safer): SpeechGPT 0.945, LLaMA-Omni 0.852, DiVA 0.752, Qwen2-Audio 0.552, Gemini-flash 0.548, GPT-4o 0.190, SALMONN 0.137

Base-vs-APT+ ASR (Table 3, lower=safer): SALMONN 0.356->0.433, Qwen2-Audio 0.491->0.526, DiVA 0.580->0.674, Gemini-flash 0.611->0.737, GPT-4o 0.235->0.314 (all shifts statistically significant, paired t-test p<0.01)
APT+ Toxicity Score shifts (Table 3, 1-5): Gemini-flash 3.084->3.431, GPT-4o 1.639->1.734, DiVA 2.314->2.428; SALMONN slightly down 2.759->2.670
SALMONN base Relevance only 1.531 (Table 2), reflecting over-refusal / low usability
Semantic safety threshold = 0.638 average Whisper-transcription similarity between clean and 13-round perturbed audio
Human study: Cohen's Kappa inter-rater agreement 0.72; audio rated unintelligible (<4/10) after ~13 cumulative noise rounds
Bayesian optimization / TPE config: 10 startup quasi-random trials, gamma=0.10 quantile, 24 EI candidates, 2D parameter space [0,1]^2
Time-stretch and fade perturbations most frequently selected by Bayesian optimization and most effective at degrading robustness (Figure 5)

APT = 7 perturbation methods across 3 domains (time: energy-distribution scaling, trimming <=0.1s, fade; frequency: pitch shifting, temporal scaling via phase vocoder; mixing: extra-auditory infra/ultrasound priming A0=0.1, natural-noise injection)
baselines_compared: Seven target LAMs evaluated: SpeechGPT, SALMONN, DiVA, Qwen2-Audio, LLaMA-Omni, Gemini-2.0-flash, GPT-4o-audio (also compared against text-only reference of each model's underlying LLM)

Prior audio-jailbreak methods contrasted qualitatively in Table 1: Yang et al. 2024 (350 samples, spelled-letter audio), Hughes et al. 2024 (BoN, 159 samples, text-level only), Xiao et al. 2025 (520 samples, TTS tone/speed edits) - all lacking semantic-preservation constraints and open benchmark release
Referenced-but-not-reimplemented prior attacks: Shen et al. 2024 (naive text-to-audio transfer), AdvWave/Kang et al. 2024 (white-box dual-phase attack on open-source models)
